habitat: Disturbed ground, field margins, and rocky Mediterranean scrub
blurb: The classic cool-season edible climber — delicate compound leaves, white or purple pea flowers, and fat green pods ready to pick from spring through early summer. Direct-sow as early as March on the Front Range; it thrives in our cool spring air and needs a trellis or twiggy sticks to scramble up.
size: Dwarf types 1–2 ft; tall types 4–6 ft tall × 1–2 ft wide
water: Moderate; keep evenly moist throughout the season — not xeric; mulch to keep roots cool in our warm spring
spread: Climbs by branched tendrils; needs a trellis, netting, or twiggy sticks; does not spread or self-sow aggressively on the Front Range
seasons: Cool-spring foliage, flowers, and pods (March–June on the Front Range); declines and dies down as summer heat builds; no winter presence
wildlife: Self-pollinating but bumblebees visit; a nitrogen-fixing legume that enriches the soil for follow-on crops
deer: Not reliably deer-resistant — tender new growth and pods may be browsed; bird netting also protects seedlings from pigeons
toxic: Edible and non-toxic; do not confuse with ornamental sweet pea (Lathyrus odoratus), a separate plant in this guide that is toxic

care.hardiness: Grown as a cool-season annual — seedlings tolerate light frost and germinate in soil as cold as 40°F, but the plant completes its life in one season and declines abruptly once temperatures push past about 85°F.[1][2] On the Front Range (USDA zones 5b–6a), this means a spring window of roughly March–June before the summer heat ends the show.[1]
care.planting: Direct-sow as early as late February to March on the Front Range — peas are among the first things into the garden, as soon as the soil is workable (not frozen solid or waterlogged). Seedlings tolerate light frosts, and cool soil actually suits them; trying to time them after the last frost date means missing their best growing window. Sow at the base of a trellis, netting, or teepee — they must have something to grip. A second sowing in mid-July can yield a small fall crop before killing frost in October. In containers, use a pot at least 12 inches deep and a free-draining mix, water consistently (containers dry fast in Front Range wind and heat), and keep roots shaded; potted peas finish even sooner when summer heat arrives.[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun — at least 6 hours of direct sun for the best pod set. In our high-altitude Front Range climate, morning sun with some afternoon shade can extend the cool-season window slightly and slow the heat-induced decline, but a shaded spot will sacrifice yield.[1]
care.soil: Fertile, well-drained soil with good organic matter content and a near-neutral pH (6.0–8.0). Boulder's alkaline clay is workable if generously amended with compost and kept from waterlogging. Peas are legumes and fix their own nitrogen when inoculated with the correct *Rhizobium* strain (sold as 'pea and bean inoculant'); inoculating seed at planting is a worthwhile step, especially in a new bed that has not grown legumes before.[1][2]
care.water: Moderate and consistent — keep the soil evenly moist through flowering and pod fill; dry roots during pod development cause poor fill and bitterness. Water at the base (overhead water invites mildew). Mulch the bed to keep roots cool and moisture even. Ease off only as the plant yellows and declines.[2]
care.spacing: Sow seeds 1 inch deep, 2–3 inches apart in a row, or cluster 3–4 seeds at each trellis post. Space rows 18–24 inches apart for airflow. Staggered sowings 2 weeks apart stretch the harvest season over several weeks.[1][2]
care.propagation: Seed only — direct-sow; peas have a taproot and dislike transplanting. Soak seeds overnight to speed germination; apply *Rhizobium* inoculant for the best nitrogen fixation. Peas do not reliably self-sow in our climate. Save seed from fully dried, papery pods at season's end for next year's planting.[1][2]
care.sow: Sow seed 1 inch deep, 2–3 inches apart. Germination takes 7–14 days in cool soil (40–60°F) and as few as 5 days in warmer soil. Germination stops above about 85°F.[1][2]
care.bloom: Small white or pink-purple butterfly-shaped (papilionaceous) flowers appear on axillary stalks from mid-spring. Self-pollination occurs before the flower fully opens on most varieties, though bumblebees do visit. Pods follow within days of flower drop.[1]
care.harvest: Three types of peas, each harvested differently: **shelling peas** (garden peas) — pick when pods are fat and filled but still bright green, roughly 60–70 days from sowing; the seeds inside should be sweet and tender; **snap peas** — harvest at full pod length when the pod wall is still fleshy and sweet, before the seeds push through; **snow peas** — harvest while pods are flat and the seeds barely visible (7–10 days after petal drop). Pick every 2–3 days — letting pods over-mature on the vine stops the plant from setting more pods. Harvest early morning when pods are crisp and cool.[1][2]
care.maintenance: Provide support before plants need it — insert twiggy sticks, netting, or a trellis at planting time so tendrils can grip from the start. Tall types (4–6 ft) need a sturdy trellis; dwarf types (1–2 ft) do fine with short twiggy sticks or a low cage. Pinch out the growing tip at 6–8 nodes on tall types to encourage branching and pod set. Pull and compost the spent plant (no pods on it) after it declines.[2]
care.troubles: **Powdery mildew** is the most common problem — a white floury coating on leaves in warm, dry, or crowded conditions; good airflow and avoiding overhead watering reduce it. **Pea aphids** cluster on tender shoot tips and new growth; knock off with a water spray. **Damping off** (seedlings collapsing at the soil line) happens when seed is sown in cold, wet, heavy soil — wait until it is workable, not waterlogged. **Birds** (pigeons, sparrows) love the seeds and seedlings — protect with netting until plants are 6 inches tall. **Fusarium wilt** causes sudden yellowing; avoid replanting in the same bed two years in a row. **Root rot** in waterlogged, cold soil — drainage is critical.[1][2]
care.companions: Peas combine well with carrots, turnips, radishes, cucumbers, corn, beans, and most vegetables. They add nitrogen to the soil as they grow, benefiting follow-on crops. Avoid planting near onions, garlic, gladiolus, and potatoes.[3]
care.feeding: Light feeders — peas fix their own nitrogen. A good compost amendment at bed prep is sufficient. Avoid high-nitrogen fertilizer, which pushes leafy growth at the expense of pod set. A balanced fertilizer early in growth is acceptable in very lean soils.[1]

edible.card: Seeds (shelled peas), edible pods (snap/snow), and pea shoots
edible.summary: One of the most important food plants in the world — the ripe or immature seeds (shelled peas), the whole tender pod (snap and snow peas), and the young shoot tips and leaves are all edible raw or cooked.[1][2]
edible.parts: Three distinct edible products: (1) **Shelled peas** — the seeds inside mature pods; sweet and eaten raw or cooked, fresh or frozen. (2) **Edible pods** — in snap-pea and snow-pea varieties the whole pod is eaten; snap pods are harvested rounded and full, snow peas when flat. (3) **Pea shoots** — the young tendrils and growing tips of any variety; delicate and sweet, eaten raw in salads or briefly stir-fried. The pea flower is also edible as a garnish.[1][2]
edible.preparation: Shelled garden peas: eat raw when young and sweet, or boil/steam briefly (2–4 minutes) and season simply. Frozen peas are blanch-frozen and need only a brief heat through. Snap and snow peas: eat raw, stir-fry for 1–2 minutes, or steam briefly to retain crunch. Pea shoots: eat raw in salads or wilted for 30 seconds in a hot pan. Do not confuse garden pea pods with ornamental sweet pea (Lathyrus odoratus) pods, which are TOXIC — Lathyrus grows alongside Pisum in some gardens and the pods are visually similar.[1][2]
edible.caution: Garden pea (Pisum sativum) is a safe, mainstream food with no significant toxicity. The main practical danger is **confusion with ornamental sweet pea (Lathyrus odoratus)**, which is a separate vine plant in this guide (listed as Toxic). Lathyrus pods look similar to pea pods but are poisonous — they cause lathyrism, a serious neurological syndrome. Always confirm identification by smell (garden peas smell faintly sweet/green; sweet pea flowers are intensely fragrant) and plant source before eating any pod you did not grow yourself. Large quantities of raw mature peas are mildly hard to digest (raw starch + lectins), though normal garden portions are fine.[1][2]
